Case Study · SaaS & Subscription Billing

Fixing the Moment Trust Breaks

Autodesk's most frequent, highest-severity customer complaints aren't about CAD tools — they're about not understanding what they bought, being charged without warning, and being unable to fix a mistake before money moves. I traced the failure to its root and redesigned the renewal journey around it.

UX Research Service Design Journey Mapping Service Blueprint SaaS Billing
Role
UX Researcher & Service Designer
Domain
SaaS Subscription Billing
Focus
Renewal & Cancellation Experience
Problem

Customers can't predict their own bill

Across Autodesk's Community forums, Trustpilot, and BBB complaint filings, one theme dominates every other: people don't know what they purchased, when it renews, or why they were charged. Not a rendering bug. Not a missing feature in AutoCAD or Revit — the billing system itself.

Because Autodesk's business runs on subscription renewal, this isn't a cosmetic annoyance. Every confused renewal is simultaneously a support cost, a churn risk, a chargeback risk, and, per public BBB filings, a trust and legal risk — landing at the exact moment a customer decides whether to keep paying.

The Human Stakes

Meet Alex

A
Sole studio owner · bought a 2-seat AutoCAD subscription two years ago · not a finance specialist

Alex checked out once, picked what looked like "2 years," and moved on. A year later, a renewal charge arrived that didn't match what they remembered buying. Support confirmed the invoice was technically correct — and declined to fix it, citing a return window that had already closed before Alex even knew there was anything to return.

"I thought I bought two years, not two seats — nothing about the checkout screen told me I'd picked wrong."
Research Constraints

Reading the system from the outside

No direct access to Autodesk's internal support queue or customer base was available. I reconstructed the failure pattern through systematic review of public complaint sources — Autodesk Community threads, Trustpilot reviews, and BBB filings — cross-referenced against Autodesk's own published account and billing documentation to understand the system logic actually producing the complaints, not just the complaints themselves.

Complaint pattern synthesis

Affinity-mapped recurring themes against lifecycle stage to locate where the experience breaks, rather than treating each complaint as an isolated incident.

Service documentation audit

Reviewed Autodesk's account-role definitions, co-terming rules, renewal windows, and cancellation-stage timing to reconstruct the mechanism behind the complaints.

Why Users Get Stuck

A system built for contracts, not people

Autodesk splits one subscription into three roles — Purchaser, Admin, Payer — and routes critical information to whichever role happens to hold it, not to whoever is actually responsible for the decision.

System design choiceCustomer-facing consequence
Only "Purchaser" receives billing & renewal emailsThe person managing daily use is often blind to an approaching renewal or price change
Seats and terms edited on one screen, different effective-date rulesCustomers misread what they're changing and when it takes effect
Auto-renewal on by defaultSilence is treated as consent; customers who take no action are charged
Cancellation is a 3-stage process spanning up to ~45 daysCustomers believe they've cancelled and are billed again mid-process
Support policy anchors to a fixed return windowEven provable UI-driven mistakes are refused a correction
The Solution

Same eight stages, redesigned system

Mapped current vs. future state across the full renewal lifecycle — from checkout to outcome — showing exactly where customer sentiment breaks today, and what closes each gap.

CX Strategy

Three principles behind every fix

01

Route information to whoever can act on it.

Billing status shouldn't live with a role by historical accident — renewal health should be visible to Purchaser and Admin alike.

02

Make consequence visible before it happens.

A plain-language confirmation at checkout and a pre-charge grace window turn silent defaults into informed choices.

03

Never let "done" be ambiguous.

Cancellation is a real multi-stage process — it should look like one, with visible status, not a single unverifiable chat message.

Outcome

What success looks like

Because the failure traces to specific screens and notification logic, the fix has a directly attributable business case — a rare property for a UX investment.

↓
Billing-related support ticket volume
↓
Involuntary churn at renewal
↑
Renewal-comprehension test score
↑
First-contact resolution rate

"The billing complaints were never really about pricing — they were about a system that told the wrong person the right information, at the wrong time. Once I named it as a routing problem, the fix stopped being a mystery." — Medya Asgharyan, UX Researcher & Service Designer
